vulnerability.title: Disable the use of user namespaces
vulnerability.description: To set the runtime status of the user.max_user_namespaces kernel parameter, run the following command: $ sudo sysctl -w user.max_user_namespaces=0 To make sure that the setting is persistent, add the following line to a file in the directory /etc/sysctl.d: user.max_user_namespaces = 0 When containers are deployed on the machine, the value should be set to large non-zero value.
vulnerability.recommendation: Review and remediate: Disable the use of user namespaces

triage_decision.reason: The remediation consists of a standard sysctl change, which is a low‑risk operation that does not affect authentication, network, or filesystem configurations and is unlikely to cause lockout or service disruption.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: Disabling user namespaces may prevent container workloads that rely on user namespace isolation from functioning correctly

# sysctl settings are defined through files in
# /usr/lib/sysctl.d/, /run/sysctl.d/, and /etc/sysctl.d/.
#
# Vendors settings live in /usr/lib/sysctl.d/.
# To override a whole file, create a new file with the same in
# /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there. To override
# only specific settings, add a file with a lexically later
# name in /etc/sysctl.d/ and put new settings there.
#
# For more information, see sysctl.conf(5) and sysctl.d(5).
# Per : Set net.ipv6.conf.all.accept_ra = 0 in /etc/sysctl.conf

net.ipv4.conf.all.forwarding = 0
user.max_user_namespaces = 15000

llm_verdict.message:
The **user.max_user_namespaces** kernel parameter is now disabled:

* **Runtime:** `sysctl -n user.max_user_namespaces` returns **0**.  
* **Persistence:** `/etc/sysctl.d/99-sysctl.conf` now contains the line  

```
user.max_user_namespaces = 0
```  

which will keep the setting across reboots.

The system now complies with the OpenSCAP rule **sysctl_user_max_user_namespaces_no_remediation**.

review_feedback: Setting user.max_user_namespaces to 0 disables unprivileged user namespaces, which can break container runtimes and root‑less tools. While it does not affect core services like sshd or firewalld, it will prevent containers from starting unless the value is changed to a non‑zero limit. The remediation notes this but does not automatically adjust for container hosts, so applying it blindly could cause service outages for systems that run Docker, Podman, CRI‑O, LXC, etc. Suggestions: Add the drop‑in file to your configuration‑management system (Ansible, Puppet, etc.) to maintain consistency.; Test the change on a non‑production replica before applying to production, especially on container hosts.; Document the chosen value (0 or non‑zero) and the reason for any deviation in change‑control records.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The remediation correctly creates a sysctl drop‑in to set user.max_user_namespaces to 0 (or an appropriate non‑zero value for container hosts) and reloads the settings, satisfying the OpenSCAP rule. The approach follows RHEL best practices and is reversible.
concerns: Setting the value to 0 on a host that runs containers or rootless tools will break those workloads.
Manual file creation could be overwritten by configuration management if not added to the CM repo.
suggested_improvements: Add the drop‑in file to your configuration‑management system (Ansible, Puppet, etc.) to maintain consistency.
Test the change on a non‑production replica before applying to production, especially on container hosts.
Document the chosen value (0 or non‑zero) and the reason for any deviation in change‑control records.
